text: Let him kiss me with the kiss of his mouth: for thy breasts are better than wine,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Smelling sweet of the best ointments. Thy name is as oil poured out: therefore young maidens have loved thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Draw me: we will run after thee to the odour of thy ointments. The king hath brought me into his storerooms: we will be glad and rejoice in thee, remembering thy breasts more than wine: the righteous love thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I am black but beautiful, O ye daughters of Jerusalem, as the tents of Cedar, as the curtains of Solomon.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Do not consider me that I am brown, because the sun hath altered my colour: the sons of my mother have fought against me, they have made me the keeper in the vineyards: my vineyard I have not kept.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Shew me, O thou whom my soul loveth, where thou feedest, where thou liest in the midday, lest I begin to wander after the flocks of thy companions.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If thou know not thyself, O fairest among women, go forth, and follow after the steps of the flocks, and feed thy kids beside the tents of the shepherds.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To my company of horsemen, in Pharao’s chariots, have I likened thee, O my love.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thy cheeks are beautiful as the turtledove’s, thy neck as jewels.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: We will make thee chains of gold, inlaid with silver.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: While the king was at his repose, my spikenard sent forth the odour thereof.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A bundle of myrrh is my beloved to me, he shall abide between my breasts.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A cluster of cypress my love is to me, in the vineyards of Engaddi.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Behold thou art fair, O my love, behold thou art fair, thy eyes are as those of doves.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Behold thou art fair, my beloved, and comely. Our bed is flourishing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The beams of our houses are of cedar, our rafters of cypress trees.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I am the flower of the field, and the lily of the valleys.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As the lily among thorns, so is my love among the daughters.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As the apple tree among the trees of the woods, so is my beloved among the sons. I sat down under his shadow, whom I desired: and his fruit was sweet to my palate.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He brought me into the cellar of wine, he set in order charity in me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Stay me up with flowers, compass me about with apples: because I languish with love.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: His left hand is under my head, and his right hand shall embrace me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I adjure you, O ye daughters of Jerusalem, by the roes, and the harts of the fields, that you stir not up, nor make the beloved to awake, till she please.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The voice of my beloved, behold he cometh leaping upon the mountains, skipping over the hills.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My beloved is like a roe, or a young hart. Behold he standeth behind our wall, looking through the windows, looking through the lattices.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Behold my beloved speaketh to me: Arise, make haste, my love, my dove, my beautiful one, and come.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For winter is now past, the rain is over and gone.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The flowers have appeared in our land, the time of pruning is come: the voice of the turtle is heard in our land:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The fig tree hath put forth her green figs: the vines in flower yield their sweet smell. Arise, my love, my beautiful one, and come:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My dove in the clefts of the rock, in the hollow places of the wall, shew me thy face, let thy voice sound in my ears: for thy voice is sweet, and thy face comely.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Catch us the little foxes that destroy the vines: for our vineyard hath flourished.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My beloved to me, and I to him who feedeth among the lilies,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Till the day break, and the shadows retire. Return: be like, my beloved, to a roe, or to a young hart upon the mountains of Bether.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In my bed by night I sought him whom my soul loveth: I sought him, and found him not.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I will rise, and will go about the city: in the streets and the broad ways I will seek him whom my soul loveth: I sought him, and I found him not.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The watchmen who keep the city, found me: Have you seen him, whom my soul loveth?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When I had a little passed by them, I found him whom my soul loveth: I held him: and I will not let him go, till I bring him into my mother’s house, and into the chamber of her that bore me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I adjure you, O daughters of Jerusalem, by the roes and the harts of the fields, that you stir not up, nor awake my beloved, till she please.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who is she that goeth up by the desert, as a pillar of smoke of aromatical spices, of myrrh, and frankincense, and of all the powders of the perfumer?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Behold threescore valiant ones of the most valiant of Israel, surrounded the bed of Solomon?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: All holding swords, and most expert in war: every man’s sword upon his thigh, because of fears in the night.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: King Solomon hath made him a litter of the wood of Libanus:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The pillars thereof he made of silver, the seat of gold, the going up of purple: the midst he covered with charity for the daughters of Jerusalem.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Go forth, ye daughters of Sion, and see king Solomon in the diadem, wherewith his mother crowned him in the day of his espousals, and in the day of the joy of his heart.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: How beautiful art thou, my love, how beautiful art thou! thy eyes are doves’ eyes, besides what is hid within. Thy hair is as flocks of goats, which come up from mount Galaad.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thy teeth as flocks of sheep, that are shorn, which come up from the washing, all with twins, and there is none barren among them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thy lips are as a scarlet lace: and thy speech sweet. Thy cheeks are as a piece of a pomegranate, besides that which lieth hid within.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thy neck, is as the tower of David, which is built with bulwarks: a thousand bucklers hang upon it, all the armour of valiant men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thy two breasts like two young roes that are twins, which feed among the lilies.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Till the day break, and the shadows retire, I will go to the mountain of myrrh, and to the hill of frankincense.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou art all fair, O my love, and there is not a spot in thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Come from Libanus, my spouse, come from Libanus, come: thou shalt be crowned from the top of Amana, from the top of Sanir and Hermon, from the dens of the lions, from the mountains of the leopards.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou hast wounded my heart, my sister, my spouse, thou hast wounded my heart with one of thy eyes, and with one hair of thy neck.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: How beautiful are thy breasts, my sister, my spouse! thy breasts are more beautiful than wine, and the sweet smell of thy ointments above all aromatical spices.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thy lips, my spouse, are as a dropping honeycomb, honey and milk are under thy tongue; and the smell of thy garments, as the smell of frankincense.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My sister, my spouse, is a garden enclosed, a garden enclosed, a fountain sealed up.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thy plants are a paradise of pomegranates with the fruits of the orchard. Cypress with spikenard.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Spikenard and saffron, sweet cane and cinnamon, with all the trees of Libanus, myrrh and aloes with all the chief perfumes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The fountain of gardens: the well of living waters, which run with a strong stream from Libanus.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Arise, O north wind, and come, O south wind, blow through my garden, and let the aromatical spices thereof flow.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let my beloved come into his garden, and eat the fruit of his apple trees. I am come into my garden, O my sister, my spouse, I have gathered my myrrh, with my aromatical spices: I have eaten the honeycomb with my honey, I have drunk my wine with my milk: eat, O friends, and drink, and be inebriated, my dearly beloved.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I sleep, and my heart watcheth: the voice of my beloved knocking: Open to me, my sister, my love, my dove, my undefiled: for my head is full of dew, and my locks of the drops of the nights.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I have put off my garment, how shall I put it on? I have washed my feet, how shall I defile them?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My beloved put his hand through the key hole, and my bowels were moved at his touch.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I arose up to open to my beloved: my hands dropped with myrrh, and my fingers were full of the choicest myrrh.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I opened the bolt of my door to my beloved: but he had turned aside, and was gone. My soul melted when he spoke: I sought him, and found him not: I called, and he did not answer me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The keepers that go about the city found me: they struck me: and wounded me: the keepers of the walls took away my veil from me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I adjure you, O daughters of Jerusalem, if you find my beloved, that you tell him that I languish with love.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: What manner of one is thy beloved of the beloved, O thou most beautiful among women? what manner of one is thy beloved of the beloved, that thou hast so adjured us?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My beloved is white and ruddy, chosen out of thousands.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: His head is as the finest gold: his locks as branches of palm trees, black as a raven.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: His eyes as doves upon brooks of waters, which are washed with milk, and sit beside the plentiful streams.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: His cheeks are as beds of aromatical spices set by the perfumers. His lips are as lilies dropping choice myrrh.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: His hands are turned and as of gold, full of hyacinths. His belly as of ivory, set with sapphires.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: His legs as pillars of marble, that are set upon bases of gold. His form as of Libanus, excellent as the cedars.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: His throat most sweet, and he is all lovely: such is my beloved, and he is my friend, O ye daughters of Jerusalem.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Whither is thy beloved gone, O thou most beautiful among women? whither is thy beloved turned aside, and we will seek him with thee?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My beloved is gone down into his garden, to the bed of aromatical spices, to feed in the gardens, and to gather lilies.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I to my beloved, and my beloved to me, who feedeth among the lilies.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou art beautiful, O my love, sweet and comely as Jerusalem terrible as an army set in array.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Turn away thy eyes from me, for they have made me flee away. Thy hair is as a flock of goats, that appear from Galaad.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thy teeth as a flock of sheep, which come up from the washing, all with twins, and there is none barren among them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thy cheeks are as the bark of a pomegranate, beside what is hidden within thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There are threescore queens, and fourscore concubines, and young maidens without number.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: One is my dove, my perfect one is but one, she is the only one of her mother, the chosen of her that bore her. The daughters saw her, and declared her most blessed: the queens and concubines, and they praised her.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who is she that cometh forth as the morning rising, fair as the moon, bright as the sun, terrible as an army set in array?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I went down into the garden of nuts, to see the fruits of the valleys, and to look if the vineyard had flourished, and the pomegranates budded.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I knew not: my soul troubled me for the chariots of Aminadab.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Return, return, O Sulamitess: return, return that we may behold thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: What shalt thou see in the Sulamitess but the companies of camps? How beautiful are thy steps in shoes, O prince’s daughter! The joints of thy thighs are like jewels, that are made by the hand of a skilful workman.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thy navel is like a round bowl never wanting cups. Thy belly is like a heap of wheat, set about with lilies.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thy two breasts are like two young roes that are twins.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thy neck as a tower of ivory. Thy eyes like the fishpools in Hesebon, which are in the gate of the daughter of the multitude. Thy nose is as the tower of Libanus, that looketh toward Damascus.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thy head is like Carmel: and the hairs of thy head as the purple of the king bound in the channels.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: How beautiful art thou, and how comely, my dearest, in delights!

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thy stature is like to a palm tree, and thy breasts to clusters of grapes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I said: I will go up into the palm tree, and will take hold of the fruit thereof: and thy breasts shall be as the clusters of the vine: and the odour of thy mouth like apples.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thy throat like the best wine, worthy for my beloved to drink, and for his lips and his teeth to ruminate.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I to my beloved, and his turning is towards me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Come, my beloved, let us go forth into the field, let us abide in the villages.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let us get up early to the vineyards, let us see if the vineyard flourish, if the flowers be ready to bring forth fruits, if the pomegranates flourish: there will I give thee my breasts.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The mandrakes give a smell. In our gates are all fruits: the new and the old, my beloved, I have kept for thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who shall give thee to me for my brother, sucking the breasts of my mother, that I may find thee without, and kiss thee, and now no man may despise me?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I will take hold of thee, and bring thee into my mother’s house: there thou shalt teach me, and I will give thee a cup of spiced wine and new wine of my pomegranates.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: His left hand under my head, and his right hand shall embrace me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I adjure you, O daughters of Jerusalem, that you stir not up, nor awake my love till she please.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who is this that cometh up from the desert, flowing with delights, leaning upon her beloved? Under the apple tree I raised thee up: there thy mother was corrupted, there she was defloured that bore thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Put me as a seal upon thy heart, as a seal upon thy arm, for love is strong as death, jealousy as hard as hell, the lamps thereof are fire and flames.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Many waters cannot quench charity, neither can the floods drown it: if a man should give all the substance of his house for love, he shall despise it as nothing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Our sister is little, and hath no breasts. What shall we do to our sister in the day when she is to be spoken to?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If she be a wall: let us build upon it bulwarks of silver: if she be a door, let us join it together with boards of cedar.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I am a wall: and my breasts are as a tower since I am become in his presence as one finding peace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The peaceable had a vineyard, in that which hath people: he let out the same to keepers, every man bringeth for the fruit thereof a thousand pieces of silver.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My vineyard is before me. A thousand are for thee, the peaceable, and two hundred for them that keep the fruit thereof.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou that dwellest in the gardens, the friends hearken: make me hear thy voice.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Flee away, O my beloved, and be like to the roe, and to the young hart upon the mountains of aromatical spices.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner
